questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis in liver is:
questions.explanation: Phosphofructokinase-1 catalyzes fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate and is the committed, rate-limiting step of glycolysis. Hexokinase and glucokinase catalyze the first step but are not the main committed regulatory enzyme. Pyruvate kinase is another regulated step but not the principal rate-limiting step. A common trap is confusing the first step with the committed step.

questions.questionText: A child with fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and accumulation of glycogen with normal structure most likely has deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Hers disease is due to liver glycogen phosphorylase deficiency and leads to hepatomegaly and mild fasting hypoglycemia with relatively normal glycogen structure. Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency causes Von Gierke disease with more severe lactic acidosis and hyperuricemia. Debranching enzyme deficiency causes Cori disease with abnormal glycogen. Branching enzyme deficiency causes Andersen disease with poorly branched glycogen and progressive cirrhosis.
questions.source: Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, Glycogen Metabolism

questions.questionText: Which enzyme of gluconeogenesis requires biotin as a cofactor?
questions.explanation: Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate in mitochondria and requires biotin. PEPCK uses GTP, not biotin. Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase and glucose-6-phosphatase do not use biotin. This is a classic cofactor-enzyme association question and a common source of confusion with other carboxylases.
